title: Natural Gas Leak
steps: If you smell gas, do NOT turn on or off any lights or electrical appliances.
Do NOT use cell phones or landlines inside the building.
Open all windows and doors to ventilate the area.
Evacuate everyone from the building immediately.
Call emergency services and your gas utility provider from a safe distance outside.
Do not re-enter the building until it is declared safe by professionals.

title: Earthquake
steps: DROP to the ground before the earthquake drops you.
COVER your head and neck with your arms and seek shelter under a sturdy table.
HOLD ON to your shelter until the shaking stops.
If outdoors, move to an open area away from buildings, trees, and power lines.
If in a vehicle, stop as quickly as safety permits and stay inside.
Be prepared for aftershocks.

title: Flash Flood Response
steps: Move to higher ground immediately. Do NOT wait for instructions.
Avoid walking or driving through floodwaters. Just 6 inches (15 cm) of moving water can knock you down.
If your vehicle stalls in rapidly rising water, abandon it immediately and move to higher ground.
Stay away from bridges over fast-moving water, as the ground underneath can be unstable.
Turn off utilities at the main switches if safe to do so.
Avoid contact with floodwater, as it may be contaminated or contain dangerous debris.

title: Hazardous Chemical Spill
steps: Evacuate the area immediately and move upwind (direction the wind is blowing away from).
Do NOT attempt to clean up the spill unless you are specifically trained.
Seal the area to prevent others from entering.
If the chemical is on skin or eyes, flush with large amounts of water for at least 15 minutes.
Turn off ventilation systems if outdoors. If indoors, open windows away from the spill site.
Identify the substance (via label or SDS) only if safe to do so.

title: Landslide / Mudslide
steps: If you are inside, stay inside. Move to the part of the building furthest from the slope.
If you are outside, run to the nearest high ground away from the path of the slide.
If escape is impossible, curl into a tight ball and protect your head.
Listen for unusual sounds like trees cracking or boulders knocking together.
Stay away from the slide area; additional slides may occur.
Check for injured or trapped persons without entering the slide path directly.

title: Tornado / High Wind Shelter
steps: Go to a pre-designated 'safe room', basement, or the lowest level of the building.
Inner hallways and closets are the safest spots if no basement is available.
Stay away from windows, glass, and exterior walls.
Get under a sturdy piece of furniture (like a heavy table) and use your arms to protect your head and neck.
If outdoors or in a mobile home, seek shelter in a sturdy building immediately.
As a last resort, lie flat in a low-lying area (like a ditch) and cover your head.

title: Nuclear / Radiological Hazard
steps: GET INSIDE: Go to the center of a sturdy building or a basement.
STAY INSIDE: Close all windows and doors. Turn off fans and AC units.
STAY TUNED: Listen to emergency broadcasts for official instructions.
If you were outside, remove your outer layer of clothing and seal it in a plastic bag.
Shower gently with soap and water to wash away contamination (do not scrub or use hair conditioner).
Only consume sealed bottled water and food from sealed containers.

title: Extreme Heat (Heatstroke Prevention)
steps: Drink plenty of fluids (water, electrolytes) even if you don't feel thirsty. Avoid alcohol and caffeine.
Stay in air-conditioned buildings as much as possible. If home is hot, go to a library or mall.
Wear lightweight, light-colored, loose-fitting clothing.
Limit outdoor activity to when it's coolest, like morning and evening.
Check on neighbors, especially seniors and those living alone, twice a day.
NEVER leave children or pets in a parked car, even with windows cracked.

title: Extreme Cold / Winter Storm
steps: Stay indoors during the storm. If you must go out, wear several layers of loose-fitting, lightweight, warm clothing.
Keep dry. Change out of wet clothes immediately to prevent loss of body heat.
Watch for signs of frostbite: numbness, white or grayish-yellow skin, firm or waxy skin.
Watch for signs of hypothermia: shivering, exhaustion, confusion, fumbling hands, slurred speech.
If you lose power, close off unneeded rooms, stuff towels in cracks under doors, and wear layers.
Ensure space heaters are kept at least 3 feet away from anything that can burn.

title: Thunderstorm / Lightning Safety
steps: When thunder roars, go indoors. A sturdy building is the safest place.
If no building is available, a hard-topped metal vehicle with windows rolled up is a safe second choice.
Stay away from windows and doors, and stay off porches.
Do NOT use corded phones, computers, or other electrical equipment that puts you in contact with electricity.
Avoid plumbing, including sinks, baths, and faucets.
If caught outdoors, get to a low spot. Stay away from tall trees, poles, or metal objects.

title: Sandstorm / Dust Storm
steps: If driving, pull off the road as far as possible, turn off all lights (otherwise others might follow you into the shoulder), and set the emergency brake.
If you are outside, wear an N95 mask or wrap a damp cloth around your nose and mouth.
Stay indoors with all windows and doors closed. Turn off AC/ventilation systems to prevent dust entry.
Protect your eyes with goggles or glasses; avoid wearing contact lenses.
Stay in place until visibility improves. Do NOT attempt to walk through the storm.
Clean any dust from your skin and hair once the storm passes to avoid irritation.

title: Volcanic Ash Fall
steps: Stay indoors and away from 'downwind' areas of the volcano.
Close all windows, doors, and fireplace dampers. Turn off all fans and heating/cooling systems.
If you must go outside, use a mask (N95) and wear goggles to protect your eyes from abrasive ash.
Wear long-sleeved clothing and pants to minimize ash contact with skin.
Do NOT drive in heavy ash fall; ash is abrasive and can damage car engines and clog filters.
Remove heavy ash from your roof if safe to do so, as ash is heavy and can cause structural collapse when wet.
